summary: The SE BeltLine corridor is buzzing with new construction finally breaking ground in Reynoldstown while Inman Park welcomes Mushi Ni's Asian-American comfort food. Meanwhile, airport chaos dominates headlines as TSA agents work without pay and ICE provides security backup.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown's Stalled Beltline Corner Project Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: After nearly five years of sitting as an empty lot, the corner of Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing action. Embry Development has started construction on 85 residential units that were originally permitted by Toll Brothers. It's a welcome sight for anyone who's walked past that cleared corner wondering what happened to all the big development promises.
topStory.body:
If you've been walking the Eastside Trail and wondering what's been going on with that cleared corner lot near Memorial Drive, your patience is finally paying off. Construction crews are now on site at 195 Chester Avenue, where Embry Development Company has taken over a project that's been in limbo since the land was cleared back in 2021.

The backstory here is classic Atlanta development drama. National builder Toll Brothers originally bought the 2-acre site and got permits for 85 residential units (a mix of townhomes and for-sale condos), but then walked away from the project. Embry Development stepped in two years ago and has been working through the details ever since. The location is prime real estate, just a block west of the Beltline's Eastside Trail and a stone's throw from all the action on Memorial Drive.

What's going up is being called a mix of townhomes and condos, though specific pricing hasn't been released yet. Given the location and the current market, expect these to be priced for the new Reynoldstown rather than the old one. Still, it's good to see movement on a corner that's been an eyesore for way too long.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site at 195 Chester Avenue in Reynoldstown showing early groundwork for residential development

stories.headline: Decatur's 'Missing Middle' Village Launches Next Week
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is soft-launching its sales effort for 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards near Midway Road. With Decatur home prices hitting $700,000, this project aims to offer more accessible single-family options ITP. The pocket neighborhood replaces underused lots with a village-style community that could be a model for other infill projects.
stories.body:
Anyone who's been house hunting in Decatur lately knows the sticker shock is real. With median home values climbing past $700,000, finding something affordable and still technically single-family has become nearly impossible. That's where Cottages at Midway comes in, and they're starting their soft launch next week to drum up interest.

The concept is what planners call 'missing middle' housing (18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards and green space on 2.1 acres near Midway Road). It's the kind of development that gives you your own front door and yard but shares common areas with neighbors. Think of it as somewhere between a traditional subdivision and a condo complex. The location puts you close to the major commercial corridor and downtown Decatur without paying downtown Decatur prices.

This isn't just about one project, though. If Cottages at Midway works, it could be a template for how we handle infill development across the metro. Taking underused lots and turning them into walkable, community-oriented housing is exactly what we need more of, especially as land gets scarcer and prices keep climbing.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing layout of Cottages at Midway development with houses arranged around shared courtyards

stories.headline: West End's GW Parkside Townhomes Start Taking Shape
stories.summary: The townhome project at 600 Hopkins Street is finally getting some vertical action after a year of site prep. Ackerman & Co. (the same folks behind Lee + White) are bringing 23 for-sale units to this stretch of the Beltline, with the first seven coming to market this spring. It's relatively rare to see for-sale townhomes along the southwest Beltline corridor.
stories.body:
The construction fencing at 600 Hopkins Street has been up for what feels like forever, but now there's actually something to see behind it. Ackerman & Co. has been working on this West End townhome project for a year now, and the first phase of seven units is starting to show some real progress.

What makes GW Parkside (formerly called Hopkins and White Townhomes) interesting is that it's bringing for-sale townhomes to a stretch of the Beltline that's seen mostly rental apartments and condos. Ackerman & Co. knows this area well (they're the same developer behind the Lee + White complex with all its food and office offerings), so they understand the neighborhood dynamics. The location puts you right on the Westside Trail with easy access to all the activity around Lee + White and the broader West End scene.

Pricing details haven't been released yet, but given the Beltline location and the current market, expect these to command a premium. Still, it's good to see more ownership options coming online in an area that's been dominated by rentals. The first units should hit the market in the next few weeks.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhome development showing modern residential units near the Beltline

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez opened their first restaurant today on Elizabeth Street, just off the Freedom Park Trail. The pop-up-turned-restaurant serves everything from soft shell crab bao to Peking chicken platters alongside cocktails like Thai tea martinis. Both chefs bring serious credentials (Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC), and the location puts them right in the heart of walkable Inman Park.
stories.body:
The restaurant opening everyone in Inman Park has been waiting for finally happened today. Mushi Ni, the new spot from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, opened its doors on Elizabeth Street, and the menu is exactly what this neighborhood needed. We're talking Asian-American comfort food that goes way beyond your typical fusion fare.

The credentials here are legit. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, and both chefs have been testing their concept through pop-ups around the city. The menu hits all the right notes: soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, crab Rangoon puffs, and those Peking chicken platters that feed two people for $25. The cocktail program is equally thoughtful, with drinks like tamarind whiskey sours and Thai tea martinis that actually complement the food.

The location is perfect too, right off the Freedom Park Trail where you can walk off that Peking chicken dinner. Given how quickly good restaurants get discovered in Inman Park, you'll want to get in there sooner rather than later. The fact that they're doing both lunch and dinner from day one shows they're serious about becoming a neighborhood staple.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter at Mushi Ni restaurant showing traditional presentation

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Opens Smoky Outpost at Ansley Mall
stories.summary: The Austin and Charleston pitmaster John Lewis has brought his acclaimed barbecue operation to the space behind Cook's Warehouse. The setup is unlike anything in Atlanta, with six 22-foot smokers running 24/7 in a windowed loft overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. Early reports suggest this might be the best brisket you'll find in the city.
stories.body:
John Lewis didn't mess around when he decided to bring his barbecue empire to Atlanta. The new Lewis Barbecue location at Ansley Mall features what might be the most impressive smokehouse setup in Georgia: six massive 22-foot smokers (welded from 1,000-gallon propane tanks) running around the clock in a panoramic loft space that overlooks the new Beltline pedestrian bridge.

This isn't Lewis's first rodeo. He's built a serious reputation in Austin and Charleston for Texas-style barbecue that actually lives up to the hype. The Atlanta location follows the same playbook: focus obsessively on the fundamentals, run those smokers in staggered eight-hour shifts to maintain consistent output, and don't cut corners on the meat. Early tastings suggest the brisket here is going to give every other barbecue joint in town something to worry about.

The Ansley Mall location puts Lewis right in the heart of the action, with easy access from both Midtown and the Beltline corridor. Given how much good barbecue Atlanta has been missing (despite what some people claim), this could be exactly what we needed. The fact that you can watch the smoking operation through those panoramic windows just adds to the theater of it all.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue showing brisket and sides

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Transformation Nearly Done
stories.summary: The mile-long project between downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA station is in its final stages after starting construction last August. The transformation includes protected bike lanes, new sidewalks, and traffic calming measures designed to make the corridor more people-friendly. It's been nearly a decade in the planning, but the results look promising for both cyclists and pedestrians.
stories.body:
If you've been avoiding West Howard Avenue because of all the construction chaos, it's almost time to give it another look. The Complete Streets project that kicked off last August is in its final stages, and the transformation from high-speed car sewer to people-friendly corridor is pretty dramatic.

The scope of work covers the full mile from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) to the city limits at Paden Circle, right where East Lake MARTA station sits. We're talking about a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, new sidewalks, improved lighting, and traffic calming measures designed to slow down cars and make the street work for everyone. It's the kind of infrastructure investment that sounds boring but makes a huge difference in how a neighborhood functions.

This project has been nearly a decade in the making, which tells you something about how long it takes to get infrastructure changes done in metro Atlanta. But the payoff should be worth it. Connecting downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA via protected bike lanes creates a legitimate car-free option for a lot of trips, and the improved sidewalks make the whole corridor more walkable. Now we just need the rest of the metro to catch up.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lane on West Howard Avenue in Decatur with concrete barriers

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deploy to Hartsfield-Jackson Amid TSA Shortage
stories.summary: Armed federal immigration agents in tactical gear are now patrolling the world's busiest airport to help with security as the partial government shutdown creates massive delays. The agents aren't doing security screenings or immigration enforcement, just providing general security backup for overworked TSA employees. Airport officials are telling travelers to prepare for four-hour wait times.
stories.body:
The scene at Hartsfield-Jackson this week has been something else. Armed ICE agents in full tactical gear are walking the concourses, not to arrest anyone but just to provide security backup while TSA agents work without pay and many of their colleagues call out sick. It's a visible reminder of how the federal shutdown is rippling through every aspect of travel.

The numbers tell the story: nearly 3,500 TSA agents nationwide missed work on Monday alone, and Atlanta saw a 41% callout rate over the weekend. When you're dealing with the world's busiest airport and almost half your security workforce doesn't show up, things get ugly fast. Hence the four-hour wait time warnings and the deployment of ICE agents who normally handle immigration enforcement.

Mayor Dickens has been clear that these ICE agents aren't doing immigration enforcement during this deployment, they're just helping maintain order while the airport deals with unprecedented crowds and staff shortages. Still, it's a stark visual of how federal dysfunction translates into real problems for regular people trying to travel. If you're flying out of Atlanta anytime soon, pack your patience along with your carry-on.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents in tactical gear at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: 7-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in DeKalb County
stories.summary: A seven-year-old boy died overnight after accidentally shooting himself at a home on Loveless Drive in the Ellenwood area. Family members confirmed the child got hold of a gun and the weapon discharged. It's a tragic reminder of the importance of gun safety, especially in homes with children.
stories.body:
This is the kind of story that just breaks your heart. DeKalb County police responded to a home on Loveless Drive in Ellenwood during the early morning hours and found a seven-year-old boy who had accidentally shot himself. Family members at the scene told reporters the child was playing with a firearm when it went off.

Police investigators are treating this as an 'non-intended discharge of a weapon,' which is the official language for what everyone hopes was a terrible accident. The investigation drew a large crowd of grieving family and friends to the neighborhood, and you could see the impact this has had on everyone who knew this little boy.

These accidents are preventable, which makes them all the more heartbreaking. Gun safety advocates always stress the importance of secure storage, especially in homes with children, but that advice doesn't help this family now. The road was blocked off for hours during the investigation, and the community is left processing another senseless loss of young life.
stories.imageAlt: Police vehicles at scene of investigation on Loveless Drive

stories.headline: City Council Advances Resolutions to Restrict ICE Operations
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward two resolutions: one banning ICE detention facilities within city limits, another requiring Atlanta police to document ICE activities during joint operations. The full council votes April 20. Dozens of residents also criticized Mayor Dickens for not signing the firefighters' union contract that council ratified last year.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is taking a clear stance on immigration enforcement, with the Public Safety Committee unanimously advancing two resolutions designed to limit ICE operations in the city. The first would ban any new ICE detention facilities within city limits, while the second requires APD to document whenever ICE agents are present during police operations.

What made this meeting particularly interesting was the turnout. Dozens of residents showed up not just to support the ICE resolutions, but also to blast Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the firefighters' union contract. Council ratified that contract back in April 2025, but Dickens has been sitting on it ever since. The timing is awkward, given that the mayor has been trying to position himself as labor-friendly.

The ICE resolutions head to the full council for an April 20 vote, where they're expected to pass easily. It's largely symbolic (the city doesn't currently have ICE detention facilities anyway), but it sends a clear message about Atlanta's priorities. The bigger question is whether Dickens will sign the firefighters' contract or keep letting that political problem fester.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with residents in attendance

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's Eastside Food Hall Opens Mid-April
stories.summary: The creator of NYC's Gansevoort Market is bringing his third Atlanta food destination to the new Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club. Vela Park Food Works will feature six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining, opening in mid-April. It's another sign of the eastside's growing food scene beyond the usual Beltline spots.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid has been quietly building a food hall empire in Atlanta, and his next project is about to debut on the eastside. Vela Park Food Works is finishing construction now at the new rental complex near East Lake Golf Club, and the mid-April opening should add another serious dining destination to the area's growing food scene.

Montwaid's track record speaks for itself. He created New York City's legendary Gansevoort Market, and his Atlanta projects have consistently delivered on the promise of bringing multiple high-quality food concepts under one roof. Vela Park Food Works will feature six different chef-driven concepts, plus craft cocktails and what sounds like substantial outdoor dining space.

What's interesting about this location is that it's pushing the eastside food scene beyond the usual Beltline corridor suspects. The Vela Park development sits near East Lake Golf Club, which puts it in a different orbit from the O4W and Inman Park action. If Montwaid can make this work, it could help establish the broader eastside as a legitimate dining destination rather than just a few hot spots connected by the trail.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works showing modern food hall design
